---
title: "Measurement Error in Causal Inference"
author: "Bielicke, L. and Xochihua-Tlecuitl, T."
date: today
jupyter: python3
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: false
    self-contained: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{python}
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from IPython.display import display, HTML

def show(fig):
    display(HTML(fig.to_html(full_html=False, include_plotlyjs="cdn")))

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
```

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Key Ideas

By the end of this tutorial you should be able to:

1. Define *classical* measurement error 
2. Describe how classical measurement error influences treatment, conditioning,
and outcome variables differently
4. Apply instrumental variables and 2SLS to correct for measurement bias
5. TODO
6. TODO
:::

---

## 1. Measurement Error: Definitions & Types
**Measurement
error** is the misalignment between the true state of a variable and its reported
state. We often assume that the variables we reason about are the variables
we observe. This assumption rarely holds in practive. Instead, we observe some
*proxy variable*
i.e., a noisy or imperfect stand-in for the true variable of interest. 

Understanding measurement error is important because the variable we draw
conclusions *with* is not always the variable we draw conclusions *about*.
To identify unbiased causal estimates, we need to understadn the relationship
between the variable of interest and a noisy proxy. 


### 1.1 Classical Measurement Error
Classical measurement error occurs when the observed variable equals the the true value **plus**
some noise:

$$D^* = D + \varepsilon$$

where the error term $\varepsilon$ satisfies four key assumptions:

- $\mathbb{E}(\varepsilon) = 0$ (zero mean)
- $\varepsilon \perp D$ (independent of the true value)
- $\varepsilon \perp Y$ (independent of the outcome)
- $\varepsilon \perp Z$ (independent of any covariate)

The DAG below encodes the independence assumptions. 

```{dot}
//| label: fig-dag-basic
//| fig-cap: "We observe Y* (the proxy) but we are interested in Y. The error U depends only on Y*."
//| fig-height: 1.6
digraph {
  rankdir=LR
  node [fontname="Helvetica" fontsize=13]
  D      [shape=square]
  Y      [shape=circle style=dashed]
  Ystar  [shape=square  label="Y*"]
  U [shape=circle  style=dashed label="U" width=0.45 height=0.45 fixedsize=true fontsize=10]
  D -> Y
  Y -> Ystar
  U -> Ystar [style=dashed]
}
```

There are other types of measurmemnt error (e.g., differential). We will focus
on classicial measurment error throughout this tutorial. 

### 2 Motivating Example: Subjective Well-Being

Researchers are studying the effect of happiness on physical activity. Because
they cannot measure partipants' happiness directly, they use a self-reported to
determine happiness. The researchers believe that the self-reports, though not a
perfect representation of happiness, correlate with partipants true happiness
levels. 


::: {.callout-note}
## Note
Prior work shows that single scale surveys correlate with true metrics
at roughly $r = 0.5$, while multi-item scales reach about $r = 0.8$. Neither
is perfect!
:::

#### Other Examples of Measurement Error
- Blood pressure recorded at a single clinic visit
- Self-reported hours worked
- Human error when reading time from a clock
- Mechanical error from scales

---

## 3. Causal Estimand, Identification & Attenuation Bias

### 3.1 Causal Setup & Identification

Throughout the tutorial, we are interested in measuring the average treatment
effect (ATE) of $D$ on $Y$. Most of our results assume a linear regression
model. Below, we explain how measurement error in treatment, conditioning, and
outcome variables can influence findings.

### 3.2 Classical ME in Treatment: Attenuation Bias
We want to determine $\beta$ in $Y = \beta D + \varepsilon$, but we
observe only $D^* = D + U$. Plugging the proxy
into OLS gives:

$$\hat{\beta} = \frac{\text{Cov}(Y,\, D^*)}{\text{Var}(D^*)} =
\frac{\text{Cov}(\beta D + \varepsilon,\; D + U)}{\text{Var}(D) + \text{Var}(U)}
= \frac{\beta\,\text{Var}(D)}{\text{Var}(D) + \text{Var}(U)} = \beta\lambda$$

where $\lambda = \frac{\text{Var}(D)}{\text{Var}(D) + \text{Var}(U)}$ is the
**reliability ratio**. In the derivation above, we leverage the classical
ME assumptions that $\text{Cov}(D, U) = 0$ and
$\text{Cov}(\varepsilon, U) = 0$.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## In which direction is $\hat{\beta}$ biased when there is classical
measurement error in $D$ (hint: what is the value of $\lambda$)?

Because $\lambda < 1$, the OLS estimate attenuates toward zero. For example,
when  $\lambda =
0.5$, a true effect of $\beta = 0.6$ appears as $\hat{\beta} \approx 0.3$.
:::

::: {.callout-tip} ## Attenuation Bias Attenuation bias occurs when a
regressor's estimated coefficient is biased toward zero. Mesurement error on
treatment variables results in attenuation bias. 
:::

Explore the chart below to see how the reliability ratio affects our causal
estimate. Notice that when the reliability ratio gets smaller, the fitted slope
shrinks toward zero even as the true relationship stays fixed. In other words,
the causal estimate is attenuated.

```{python}
#| label: fig-attenuation
#| fig-cap: "Attenuation bias as reliability decreases. The green line is the true slope; the red dashed line is what OLS recovers from the noisy proxy."

n = 100
beta_true = 0.6
lambdas = np.round(np.arange(0.10, 1.01, 0.05), 2)
dline = np.linspace(-3.5, 3.5, 120)

frames, steps = [], []
for lam in lambdas:
    var_u = (1 - lam) / lam
    D_true = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
    U      = rng.normal(0, np.sqrt(var_u), n)
    D_obs  = D_true + U
    Y      = beta_true * D_true + rng.normal(0, 1, n)
    b_hat  = np.cov(Y, D_obs)[0, 1] / np.var(D_obs)
    b_th   = beta_true * lam

    frames.append(go.Frame(
        name=str(lam),
        data=[
            go.Scatter(x=D_obs, y=Y, mode="markers",
                       marker=dict(size=3, color="steelblue", opacity=0.25),
                       showlegend=False),
            go.Scatter(x=dline, y=beta_true * dline, mode="lines",
                       name=f"True β = {beta_true}",
                       line=dict(color="green", width=2.5)),
            go.Scatter(x=dline, y=b_hat * dline, mode="lines",
                       name=f"OLS β̂ = {b_hat:.3f}",
                       line=dict(color="crimson", width=2.5, dash="dash")),
            go.Scatter(x=dline, y=b_th * dline, mode="lines",
                       name=f"Theory βλ = {b_th:.3f}",
                       line=dict(color="darkorange", width=1.5, dash="dot")),
        ]
    ))
    steps.append(dict(
        method="animate",
        label=str(lam),
        args=[[str(lam)], dict(mode="immediate",
                               frame=dict(duration=0, redraw=True),
                               transition=dict(duration=0))]
    ))

# start at λ=1.0 (last frame)
init = frames[-1].data
fig_att = go.Figure(data=init, frames=frames)
fig_att.update_layout(
    xaxis=dict(title="Observed D*", range=[-4, 4]),
    yaxis=dict(title="Y", range=[-4, 4]),
    legend=dict(x=0.02, y=0.98, bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.7)"),
    margin=dict(t=40, b=80),
    sliders=[dict(
        active=len(lambdas) - 1,
        currentvalue=dict(prefix="Reliability λ = ", visible=True, xanchor="center"),
        pad=dict(b=10, t=40),
        steps=steps
    )],
    height=440,
)
show(fig_att)
```

### 3.3 ME in Outcome Variables
We *still* want to determine $\beta$ in $Y = \beta D + \varepsilon$. This time, we
observe a noisy outcome $Y^* = Y + U$. Plugging the proxy
into OLS gives:

$$Y^* = \beta D + (\varepsilon + U)$$

The measurement noise is absorbed entirely into the composite residual
$\varepsilon + U$. Because $U$ is independent of $D$, the relationship between
$D$ and $Y$ is unbiased. However, the residual variance increases from
$\sigma^2_\varepsilon$ to $\sigma^2_\varepsilon + \sigma^2_U$, which inflates
standard errors and reduces $R^2$.

### Exercise: Outcome measurement error
The following exersize shows how analysis with a noisy measure of $Y$ compares
to analysis with the ground truth measure. 

The `NHANES` dataset records average systolic blood pressure ($Y$, in mmHg) and
age ($D$, in years) for 8,551 adults. `BPSysAve` is the mean of three readings
taken in the same visit. For the following exercise, we assume these values are the ground truth blood
pressure and  simulate a noisy measure: 


$$\text{BP}^*_i = \text{BP}_i + U_i, \quad U_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\,15^2)$$

<details>
<summary>Load the data in R</summary>

```r
library(NHANES)
d <- na.omit(NHANES[, c("BPSysAve", "Age")])
set.seed(5)
d$BP_noisy <- d$BPSysAve + rnorm(nrow(d), mean = 0, sd = 15)
```

</details>
**Question 1.** Compute the measurement error $U = Y^* - Y$ (`BP_noisy` minus
`BPSysAve`). What is the mean and standard deviation?

```r
err <- d$BP_noisy - d$BPSysAve
mean(err)
sd(err)
```

**Question 2.** Check the correlation between the original `BPSysAve` and
`BP_noisy`.

```r
cor(d$BPSysAve, d$BP_noisy)
```

**Question 3.** Regress systolic blood pressure on age using the original
`BPSysAve` and then using `BP_noisy`. Compare the slope coefficients, standard
errors, and $R^2$ across the two models. 

```r
summary(lm(BPSysAve ~ Age, data = d))
summary(lm(BP_noisy ~ Age, data = d))
```

::: {.callout-tip icon=false collapse=true}
## Solution

```r
library(NHANES)
d <- na.omit(NHANES[, c("BPSysAve", "Age")])
set.seed(5)
d$BP_noisy <- d$BPSysAve + rnorm(nrow(d), mean = 0, sd = 15)

# error stats
err <- d$BP_noisy - d$BPSysAve
mean(err)   # ≈ 0.0  
sd(err)     # ≈ 15.1

# correlation
cor(d$BPSysAve, d$BP_noisy)   # ≈ 0.752

# regression
summary(lm(BPSysAve ~ Age, data = d))
# slope = 0.439,  SE = 0.0079,  R² = 0.264

summary(lm(BP_noisy ~ Age, data = d))
# slope = 0.440,  SE = 0.0113,  R² = 0.151
```
:::

The two slopes are nearly identical (0.439 vs 0.440). The difference is due to
sampling noise. The standard error grows from 0.0079 to 0.0113 (+43%) and $R^2$
falls from 0.264 to 0.151. A noisier outcome variable means you need a larger
sample to detect the same true effect.

### Simulation

Each point below is one observation plotted as (true $Y$, observed $Y^*$). Drag
the slider to add noise to the outcome variable. Notice that the mean stays near zero while
the standard deviation grows.

```{python}
#| label: fig-outcome-me
#| fig-cap: "Classical ME in the outcome: true Y (x-axis) vs observed Y* (y-axis). The error mean stays ~0 while the SD grows with noise."

n = 100
beta_true = 0.6
sigma_y_vals = np.round(np.arange(0.0, 2.05, 0.1), 1)
D_ome = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
eps_ome = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
Y_true_ome = beta_true * D_ome + eps_ome

frames_ome, steps_ome = [], []
for sigma in sigma_y_vals:
    U_Y = rng.normal(0, sigma, n) if sigma > 0 else np.zeros(n)
    Y_obs_ome = Y_true_ome + U_Y
    err_mean = float(np.mean(U_Y))
    err_sd   = float(np.std(U_Y))
    corr_yy  = float(np.corrcoef(Y_true_ome, Y_obs_ome)[0, 1])

    frames_ome.append(go.Frame(
        name=str(sigma),
        data=[
            go.Scatter(
                x=Y_true_ome, y=Y_obs_ome, mode="markers",
                marker=dict(size=4, color="steelblue", opacity=0.35),
                name="Observations", showlegend=True,
            ),
            go.Scatter(
                x=[-4, 4], y=[-4, 4], mode="lines",
                name="Perfect measurement",
                line=dict(color="gray", width=1.5, dash="dash"),
                showlegend=True,
            ),
            go.Scatter(
                x=[None], y=[None], mode="markers",
                marker=dict(opacity=0),
                name=f"Error mean = {err_mean:+.3f}   SD = {err_sd:.3f}   r(Y, Y*) = {corr_yy:.3f}",
                showlegend=True,
            ),
        ]
    ))
    steps_ome.append(dict(
        method="animate", label=str(sigma),
        args=[[str(sigma)], dict(mode="immediate",
                                  frame=dict(duration=0, redraw=True),
                                  transition=dict(duration=0))]
    ))

fig_ome = go.Figure(data=frames_ome[0].data, frames=frames_ome)
fig_ome.update_layout(
    xaxis=dict(title="True outcome  Y", range=[-5, 5]),
    yaxis=dict(title="Observed outcome  Y*", range=[-7, 7]),
    legend=dict(x=0.02, y=0.98, bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.75)"),
    margin=dict(t=40, b=80),
    sliders=[dict(
        active=0,
        currentvalue=dict(prefix="Noise σ = ", visible=True, xanchor="center"),
        pad=dict(b=10, t=40),
        steps=steps_ome,
    )],
    height=440,
)
show(fig_ome)
```

---

## 3.4 Measurement Error in Conditioning Variables
We *still* want to determine the ATE of $D$ on $Y$. This time, we assume there
is some confounding variable $Z$. Unfortunately, We only observe a noisy $Z^* =
Z + U$ where $U_Z$ is classical ME (mean zero, independent of $D$, $Y$, and $Z$).

We want to determine $\beta_Z$ in:

$$Y = \beta_D D + \beta_Z Z + \varepsilon $$

But we only observe \beta_{Z^*} in:

$$Y = \beta_D D + \beta_{Z^*} Z^* + \varepsilon^* $$

$\beta_{Z^*}$ is biased towards zero as in the bivariate regression, but what happens to $\beta_D$? We can see directly from
the DAG structure that $\beta_D$ is biased: there is an open backdoor back from
$D$ to $Y$ through $Z$, even after conditioning on $Z^*$. 


```{dot}
//| label: fig-dag-conditioning
//| fig-cap: "ME in the conditioning variable: controlling for Z* instead of Z leaves the backdoor path X ← Z → Y partially open."
//| fig-height: 2.2
digraph {
  rankdir=LR
  node [fontname="Helvetica" fontsize=13]
  Z   [shape=circle style=dashed label="Z", width=0.9 height=0.9]
  Zs  [shape=square  label="Z*"]
  U [shape=circle  style=dashed,  width=0.45 height=0.45 fixedsize=true fontsize=10]
  X   [shape=square  label="X"]
  Y   [shape=square  label="Y"]
  Z  -> X
  Z  -> Y
  Z  -> Zs  [style=dashed label="P(z*|z)"]
  U -> Zs [style=dashed]
  X  -> Y
}
```


### 3.4.1 Bias in Multivariate OLS
What can we know about the direction of the bias and the relationship to
$\beta_Z$? Let's explore how classical ME in a *single variable* affects
coefficients in a multivariate OLS. First, we will show that $\beta_{Z^*}$ is
biased towards zero. Then, we will show that $\beta_{D}$ is biased in an
unpredictable direction. Specifically, the bias in $\beta_{D}$ depends on the
direction of $\beta_Z$. 

Remember that the variation in $Z$ left after removing the component of $Z$ explained by
$D$ is the residual variance from regressing $Z$ on $D$:

$$\sigma^2_{Z|D} = \text{Var}(Z) - \text{Var}(\hat{Z}_{|D}) \leq \sigma^2_Z$$

The attenuation factor in the multivariate model becomes

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{\sigma^2_{Z|D}}{\sigma^2_{Z|D} + \sigma^2_U}$$

and 
$$\hat\beta_{Z^*} = \lambda_1\,\beta_Z$$

Because $\sigma^2_{Z|D} \leq \sigma^2_Z$ and $Z$ is correlated with $D$, we have
$\lambda_1 \leq \lambda$. This relationship reveals that classicial measurement
error in multivariate regression accentuates attenuation in the variable with
ME. 


Now, we will explore how this bias in $\beta_Z$ affects $\beta_D$. To do so, we need to decompose the bias in $\hat\beta_{D} = \beta_D + \text{bias}$. Intuitively, we need to know the relationship between $D$ and $Z$ and the relationship between $Z$ and $Z^*$. The combination of these relationships tells us how much unaccounted confounding influences $\hat\beta_D$.

Let $\delta$ be the slope from the regression of $Z$ (some confounding variable) on $D$ (i.e., $Z = \delta_0 + \delta D + \epsilon$). Then:

$$\hat\beta_{D} = \beta_D + (\beta_Z - \beta_{Z^*}) \delta = \beta_D + (1 - \lambda_1)\,\beta_Z\,\delta$$

::: {.callout-note}
## Exercise

Recall that classical measurement error in a multivariate regression biases
both the mismeasured coefficient and the treatment coefficient. Specifically:

$$\hat\beta_{D} = \beta_D + (1 - \lambda_1)\,\beta_Z\,\delta$$

1. What are the two components in the bias term $(1 - \lambda_1)\beta_Z\delta$?

2. What does $\delta$ represent? When is this term large?

3. What does $(1 - \lambda_1)\beta_Z$ represent? How does the sign of $\beta_Z$
affect the direction of bias in $\beta_D$?
:::

$\delta$ tells us the strength of the relationship between $Z$ and $D$.
$(1 - \lambda_1)\beta_Z$ is the bias introduced by measurement error. The bias
term $(1 - \lambda_1)\beta_Z\delta$ is non-zero whenever $D$ and $Z$ are
correlated ($\delta \neq 0$) and $Z$ has a true effect ($\beta_Z \neq 0$). In
other words, measurement error in a conditioning variable biases the treatment
coefficient, and the direction of bias depends on $\beta_Z$ and $\delta$.

Explore the graph below to see how measurement error in a conditioning variable
influence $\beta_D$. The starting DGP is $Y = \beta_D D + \beta_Z Z +
\varepsilon$ with $\beta_D = 1$ and $\beta_Z = 0.8$. 

Try adjusting $\beta_Z$ using the dropdown menu. What happens to
$\hat{\beta}_X$ when you flip the sign of $\beta_Z$? In which direction is
$\hat{\beta}_X$ biased for different values of $\beta_Z$?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Answer

The bias on $\hat{\beta}_X$ is $(1 - \lambda_1)\beta_Z\delta$, so the
direction of bias depends on the sign of $\beta_Z$ and $\delta$. 

:::

```{python}
#| label: fig-conditioning-me
#| fig-cap: "ME in the conditioning variable. Use the dropdown to change β_Z in the DGP; drag the slider to change the reliability of Z*. Green = true β_D = 1, red = OLS estimate, orange = theoretical prediction."

n       = 200
bD_true = 1.0
rho     = 0.7

rng2 = np.random.default_rng(7)
DZ   = rng2.multivariate_normal([0, 0], [[1, rho], [rho, 1]], n)
D_c  = DZ[:, 0]
Z_c  = DZ[:, 1]
eps  = rng2.normal(0, 1, n)   # fixed noise — same draw for every bZ

# Fixed ME noise for each lambda level so changing bZ doesn't reshuffle noise
rng3 = np.random.default_rng(42)
bz_vals   = [-0.8, -0.4, 0.0, 0.4, 0.8, 1.2, 1.6]
lambdas_c = np.round(np.arange(0.1, 1.01, 0.1), 2)
sigma2_ZD = 1 - rho**2
dline_c   = np.linspace(-3.5, 3.5, 120)

lam_noise = {}
for lam in lambdas_c:
    var_u = (1 - lam) / lam if lam < 1.0 else 0.0
    lam_noise[float(lam)] = rng3.normal(0, np.sqrt(max(var_u, 1e-9)), n)

def make_traces(bz, lam, D, Z, eps_arr):
    Y_bz   = bD_true * D + bz * Z + eps_arr
    var_u  = (1 - lam) / lam if lam < 1.0 else 0.0
    U_c    = lam_noise[float(lam)] if lam < 1.0 else np.zeros(n)
    Zs     = Z + U_c
    A      = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), D, Zs])
    coefs  = np.linalg.lstsq(A, Y_bz, rcond=None)[0]
    bD_hat = float(coefs[1])
    lam1   = sigma2_ZD / (sigma2_ZD + var_u) if var_u > 0 else 1.0
    bD_th  = bD_true + (1 - lam1) * bz * rho
    return Y_bz, bD_hat, bD_th

def build_frame_data(bz, lam):
    Y_bz, bD_hat, bD_th = make_traces(bz, lam, D_c, Z_c, eps)
    return [
        go.Scatter(x=D_c, y=Y_bz, mode="markers",
                   marker=dict(size=3, color="steelblue", opacity=0.12),
                   showlegend=False),
        go.Scatter(x=dline_c, y=bD_true * dline_c, mode="lines",
                   name=f"True β_D = {bD_true}",
                   line=dict(color="green", width=2.5)),
        go.Scatter(x=dline_c, y=bD_hat * dline_c, mode="lines",
                   name=f"OLS β̂_D = {bD_hat:.3f}",
                   line=dict(color="crimson", width=2.5, dash="dash")),
        go.Scatter(x=dline_c, y=bD_th * dline_c, mode="lines",
                   name=f"Theory = {bD_th:.3f}",
                   line=dict(color="darkorange", width=1.5, dash="dot")),
    ]

animate_args = dict(mode="immediate", frame=dict(duration=0, redraw=True),
                    transition=dict(duration=0))

all_frames = []
for bzi, bz in enumerate(bz_vals):
    lam_steps = []
    for lami, lam in enumerate(lambdas_c):
        fname = f"bz{bzi}_lam{lami}"
        all_frames.append(go.Frame(name=fname, data=build_frame_data(bz, lam)))
        lam_steps.append(dict(
            method="animate", label=str(lam),
            args=[[fname], animate_args]
        ))
    # Transition frame: animating here resets lambda slider to lam=1.0 via layout
    lam_full_idx = len(lambdas_c) - 1
    all_frames.append(go.Frame(
        name=f"bz{bzi}_init",
        data=build_frame_data(bz, lambdas_c[lam_full_idx]),
        layout=dict(sliders=[dict(
            active=lam_full_idx,
            currentvalue=dict(prefix="Reliability λ(Z*) = ", visible=True, xanchor="center"),
            pad=dict(b=10, t=40),
            steps=lam_steps,
        )])
    ))

default_bzi = bz_vals.index(0.8)
init_lam_steps = [
    dict(method="animate", label=str(lam),
         args=[[f"bz{default_bzi}_lam{lami}"], animate_args])
    for lami, lam in enumerate(lambdas_c)
]

bz_buttons = [
    dict(label=f"β_Z = {bz:+.1f}", method="animate",
         args=[[f"bz{bzi}_init"], animate_args])
    for bzi, bz in enumerate(bz_vals)
]

default_frame = next(f for f in all_frames
                     if f.name == f"bz{default_bzi}_lam{len(lambdas_c)-1}")

fig_c = go.Figure(
    data=default_frame.data,
    frames=all_frames,
    layout=go.Layout(
        xaxis=dict(title="D (Treatment)", range=[-4, 4]),
        yaxis=dict(title="Y", range=[-8, 8]),
        legend=dict(x=0.02, y=0.98, bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.7)"),
        margin=dict(t=80, b=100),
        height=500,
        updatemenus=[dict(
            type="dropdown",
            direction="down",
            x=0.0, xanchor="left",
            y=1.18, yanchor="top",
            showactive=True,
            active=default_bzi,
            buttons=bz_buttons,
        )],
        sliders=[dict(
            active=len(lambdas_c) - 1,
            currentvalue=dict(prefix="Reliability λ(Z*) = ", visible=True, xanchor="center"),
            pad=dict(b=10, t=40),
            steps=init_lam_steps,
        )],
    )
)
show(fig_c)
```

---

## 5. Instrumental Variables (IV) & 2SLS
Below, we will show how using instrumental
variables can recover unbiased estimates when there is (i) classical ME in the
treatment variable and (ii) classical ME in a confounding variable. 

### IV for ME in Treatment Variables
To recover $\beta$ consistently despite noisy $D^*$, we need a second proxy
$T$ for the true $D$. The IV must satisfy three assumptions: 

1. **Exclusion**: $\text{Cov}(T, \varepsilon) = 0$ ($T$ affects $Y$ only
   through $D$)
2. **Exogeneity**: $U_D \perp T$ (measurement error in $D^*$ is independent of
the instrument $T$)
3. **Relevance**: $\text{Cov}(T, D^*) \neq 0$ ($T$ is correlated with
   the mismeasured treatment)

Remember that we want to determine $\beta$ in $Y =
\beta D + \varepsilon$, but we measure a noisy $D^* = D + U$. Then 

$$\text{Cov}(Y, T) = \text{Cov}(\beta D + \varepsilon, T) = \beta \text{Cov}(D,
T) $$

and 

$$\text{Cov}(D^*, T) = \text{Cov}(D + U_D, T) =\text{Cov}(D,
T) $$

so $\hat{\beta} = \frac{\text{Cov}(Y, T)}{\text{Cov}(D^*, T)}$. 

In the first derivation, we leverage the exclusion assumption; in the second,
we leverage the exogeneity assumption. 

### Two-Stage Least Sqaures
In practice,
we can implement the estimator as a two-stage OLS (2SLS):

**Stage 1**: regress $D^*$ on $T$:

$$\hat D^* = \hat\pi_0 + \hat\pi_1 T$$

**Stage 2**: regress $Y$ on $\hat D^*$:

$$Y = \alpha + \beta\,\hat D^* + \eta$$

### Exercise: BMI and Blood Pressure Below, you'll use 2SLS to see how
instrumental variables can recover $\beta_D$. We will compare
the results from a 2SLS regression to a regression with the ground truth
treatment. 

Suppose a researcher wants to quantify how much systolic blood pressure rises
per unit increase in BMI. The researcher suspects that reported BMI is subject
to measurement error due to scale calibration or rounding.

We treat the NHANES BMI column as ground truth and simulate two independent
noisy readings:

$$\text{BMI}^*_1 = \text{BMI} + U_1, \quad U_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\,4^2)$$
$$\text{BMI}^*_2 = \text{BMI} + U_2, \quad U_2 \sim \mathcal{N}(1,\,10^2)$$

$\text{BMI}^*_1$ is the mismeasured proxy while $\text{BMI}^*_2$ is the
instrument. 

#### Step 0: Load data and simulate measurement error

```r
library(NHANES)
d <- na.omit(NHANES[, c("BPSysAve", "BMI")])

set.seed(9)
d$bmi_star <- d$BMI + rnorm(nrow(d), mean = 0, sd = 4)   # noisy regressor X*
d$iv       <- d$BMI + rnorm(nrow(d), mean = 1, sd = 10)  # instrument
```

#### Step 1: OLS benchmarks

```r
# Benchmark: OLS on true (unobserved) BMI
ols_true <- lm(BPSysAve ~ BMI, data = d)

# What we observe in practice: OLS on the noisy proxy
ols_fit  <- lm(BPSysAve ~ bmi_star, data = d)

summary(ols_true)
summary(ols_fit)
```

#### Step 2: 2SLS with `ivreg()`

```r
library(AER)
iv_fit <- ivreg(BPSysAve ~ bmi_star | iv, data = d)
summary(iv_fit, diagnostics = TRUE)
```

#### Step 3: Compare all three

```r
models <- list(
  "OLS (true BMI)"  = ols_true,
  "OLS (noisy BMI)" = ols_fit,
  "2SLS (ivreg)"    = iv_fit
)

results <- sapply(models, function(m) {
  b  <- round(coef(m)[2], 3)        # slope (2nd coef after intercept)
  se <- round(sqrt(diag(vcov(m)))[2], 3)
  c(estimate = b, se = se)
})

print(results)
```

::: {.callout-tip icon=false collapse=true}
## Expected results

| | OLS (true BMI) | OLS (noisy BMI) | 2SLS (`ivreg`) |
|---|---|---|---|
| BMI coefficient | 0.644 | ~0.499 | ~0.644 |
| SE | (0.028) | (0.024) | (~0.043) |

OLS on the noisy proxy attenuates the coefficient from 0.644 to ~0.499. The
reliability ratio is $\lambda = 7.4^2 / (7.4^2 + 4^2) \approx 0.77$. The 2SLS
recovers the true slope despite using noisy proxies. 
:::


### 4.2 Proxy Variables as Instruments for an Unobserved Confounder
We can also use instrumental variables to recover unbiased effects when there is
classical ME in a confounding variable.

Consider the structural model below, where $Z$ is the latent (never-observed)
confounder, $W$ and $V$ are two independent observed proxies for $Z$, and
$\beta_0$ is the causal effect we want to estimate:

$$
Y = \beta_0 D + \beta_2 Z + \varepsilon_Y, \qquad
D = \beta_1 Z + \varepsilon_D, \qquad
W = \beta_3 Z + \varepsilon_W, \qquad
V = \beta_4 Z + \varepsilon_V
$$

```{dot}
//| label: fig-dag-proxy-iv
//| fig-cap: "Proxy-variable IV setup. Z is the unobserved confounder; W and V are two independent noisy proxies of Z. V acts as an instrument: it affects D and Y only through Z, and its noise ε_V is independent of ε_W."
//| fig-height: 2.2
digraph {
  rankdir=LR
  node [fontname="Helvetica" fontsize=13]
  Z  [shape=circle style=dashed]
  D  [shape=square]
  Y  [shape=square]
  W  [shape=square]
  V  [shape=square]
  eW [shape=circle style=dashed label="ε_W" width=0.45 height=0.45 fixedsize=true fontsize=10]
  eV [shape=circle style=dashed label="ε_V" width=0.45 height=0.45 fixedsize=true fontsize=10]
  { rank=same; W; D; V }
  Z -> D  [label="β₁"]
  Z -> Y  [label="β₂"]
  Z -> W  [label="β₃"]
  Z -> V  [label="β₄"]
  D -> Y  [label="β₀"]
  eW -> W [style=dashed]
  eV -> V [style=dashed]
}
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}

**Key Covariances:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cov}(D, W) &= \beta_1 \beta_3 \text{Var}(Z) \\
\text{Cov}(W, Y) &= \beta_3(\beta_0 \beta_1 + \beta_2)\text{Var}(Z) \\
\text{Cov}(D, Y) &= \beta_0\text{Var}(D) + \beta_1 \beta_2 \text{Var}(Z)
\end{aligned}$$

**Derivation of Equation (10):**

Substitute $\beta_1\text{Var}(Z) = \text{Cov}(D,W) / \beta_3$ and
$\beta_2\text{Var}(Z) = \text{Cov}(W,Y) / \beta_3 - \beta_0 \beta_1\text{Var}(Z)$
to eliminate $\beta_1 \beta_2 \text{Var}(Z)$ in $\text{Cov}(D, Y)$:

$$\beta_1 \beta_2 \text{Var}(Z) = \frac{\text{Cov}(D,W)\,\text{Cov}(W,Y)}{\beta_3^2\,\text{Var}(Z)} -
\beta_0\frac{\text{Cov}^2(D,W)}{\beta_3^2\,\text{Var}(Z)}$$

Substituting back and collecting $\beta_0$ terms:

$$\text{Cov}(D,Y) - \frac{\text{Cov}(D,W)\,\text{Cov}(W,Y)}{\beta_3^2\,\text{Var}(Z)} =
\beta_0\left[\text{Var}(D) - \frac{\text{Cov}^2(D,W)}{\beta_3^2\,\text{Var}(Z)}\right]$$

so

$$\beta_0 = \frac{\text{Cov}(D,Y) -
\dfrac{\text{Cov}(D,W)\,\text{Cov}(W,Y)}{\beta_3^2\,\text{Var}(Z)}}{\text{Var}(D) -
\dfrac{\text{Cov}^2(D,W)}{\beta_3^2\,\text{Var}(Z)}}$$
:::

If we express $\beta_0$ in terms of the variances and covariances of the
*observed* variables, we get

$$ \beta_0 = \frac{\text{Cov}(D,Y) -
\dfrac{\text{Cov}(D,W)\,\text{Cov}(W,Y)}{\beta_3^2\,\text{Var}(Z)}}{\text{Var}(D)
- \dfrac{\text{Cov}^2(D,W)}{\beta_3^2\,\text{Var}(Z)}} \tag{10} $$

but we don't observe $\beta_3^2 \text{Var}(Z)$. With the second proxy $V$ we can
identify $\beta_3^2$ \text{Var}(Z) =
\dfrac{\text{Cov}(D,W)\,\text{Cov}(W,V)}{\text{Cov}(D,V)}$. Substituting into
(10) yields an estimator that depends only on observed covariances:

$$\beta_0 = \frac{\text{Cov}(D,Y)\,\text{Cov}(D,V) -
\text{Cov}(Y,W)\,\text{Cov}(W,V)}{\text{Cov}(D,V)\,\text{Var}(D) -
\text{Cov}(D,W)\,\text{Cov}(W,V)} \tag{12}$$

---

## 6. Latent Variable Methods: SEM, Scoring & IRT


---

## 7. Measurement Invariance

---

## 8. Takeaways

- Measurement error is not a nuisance to note in a limitations section; it is a
  structural feature of the data-generating process with identifiable causal
  consequences.
- Classical error in the *treatment* attenuates coefficients toward zero by the
  reliability ratio $\lambda$. Classical error in the *outcome* inflates standard
  errors but leaves point estimates unbiased.
- Error in a *conditioning variable* leaves an open backdoor path and can bias
  estimates in either direction.
- Instrumental variables and SEM each offer consistent estimation under different
  assumptions. The choice depends on whether a valid second proxy or a full
  measurement model is available.
- Before worrying about confounding or external validity, ask whether the measures
  themselves are valid proxies for the constructs of interest.

---

## Sources

An, L. (2025). *Measurement in K-12 policy analysis* (Doctoral dissertation, Harvard University). <https://dash.harvard.edu/server/api/core/bitstreams/7035e654-685f-4d1a-837e-e66ab492c665/content>

Bulbulia, J. A. (2024). Methods in causal inference. Part 3: Measurement error and external validity threats. *Evolutionary Human Sciences*, *6*, e42. <https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4723683/pdf/dyu272.pdf>

de Romémont, J. (2024). *Measurement in data science* [Lecture notes]. <https://uclspp.github.io/POLS0013/2-measurement-theory-and-error.html>

Edwards, J. K., Cole, S. R., & Westreich, D. (2015). All your data are always missing: incorporating bias due to measurement error into the potential outcomes framework. *International Journal of Epidemiology*, *44*(4), 1452–1459. <https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11588564/>

Jovanović, M. (n.d.). *Measurement error*. bmbstats Book. <https://mladenjovanovic.github.io/bmbstats-book/measurement-error.html>

Lauderdale, B. E. (in press). *Pragmatic social measurement*. <https://uclspp.github.io/POLS0013/00_textbook/pragmatic-social-measurement.pdf>

Lemieux, T. (2019). *ECON 594: Lecture 8* [Lecture notes]. University of British Columbia. <https://econ2017.sites.olt.ubc.ca/files/2019/05/pdf_594lecture8_thomas-lemieux.pdf>

Li, F. (2024). *Instrumental variables* [Lecture slides]. Duke University. <https://www2.stat.duke.edu/~fl35/teaching/640/Chapter6.1_InstrumentalVariables.pdf>

Machielsen, B. (n.d.). *Measurement error*. <https://basmachielsen.nl/posts/measurement_error>

Hanck, C., Arnold, M., Gerber, A., & Schmelzer, M. (n.d.). *Instrumental variables regression*. In *Introduction to econometrics with R* (Ch. 12). <https://www.econometrics-with-r.org/12-ivr.html>

Pearl, J. (2012). On measurement bias in causal inference. *arXiv:1203.3504*. <https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.1203.3504>

Pischke, S. (2007). *Lecture notes on measurement error* [Lecture notes]. London School of Economics. <https://econ.lse.ac.uk/staff/spischke/ec524/Merr_new.pdf>

Rubin, E. (n.d.). *ARE 212: Section 11 — Measurement error* [Course notes]. University of California, Berkeley. <https://edrub.in/ARE212/section11.html#measurement_error>

The Econometrics Blog. (n.d.). *Beyond classical measurement error*. <https://www.econometrics.blog/post/beyond-classical-measurement-error/>
